titleStmt.title: FORGETMARK: STEALTHY FINGERPRINT EMBEDDING VIA TARGETED UNLEARNING IN LANGUAGE MODELS

analytic.title: FORGETMARK: STEALTHY FINGERPRINT EMBEDDING VIA TARGETED UNLEARNING IN LANGUAGE MODELS

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=1, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=false, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], flavor=null

div.p: Existing invasive (backdoor) fingerprints suffer from high-perplexity triggers that are easily filtered, fixed response patterns exposed by heuristic detectors, and spurious activations on benign inputs. We introduce ForgetMark, a stealthy fingerprinting framework that encodes provenance via targeted unlearning. It builds a compact, human-readable key-value set with an assistant model and predictive-entropy ranking, then trains lightweight LoRA adapters to suppress the original values on their keys while preserving general capabilities; ownership is verified under black/gray-box access by aggregating likelihood and semantic evidence into a fingerprint success rate. By relying on probabilistic forgetting traces rather than fixed trigger-response patterns, ForgetMark avoids high-perplexity triggers, reduces detectability, and lowers false triggers. Across diverse architectures and settings, it achieves 100% ownership verification on fingerprinted models while maintaining standard performance, surpasses backdoor baselines in stealthiness and robustness to model merging, and remains effective under moderate incremental fine-tuning. Our code and data can be found in https://github.com/Xuzhenhua55/ForgetMark.

div.head: INTRODUCTION
div.p: Large language models (LLMs) and related paradigms such as vision-language models [1,2,3], autonomous agents [4,5,6], and AI systems for security applications [7,8,9,10,11,12] have become central to modern machine learning research and deployment, yet their creation depends on substantial proprietary data, compute, and engineering. Illicit model exfiltration and unauthorized fine-tuning have lowered the barrier to redistributing derivatives, creating urgent needs for reliable provenance auditing and copyright protection.

Model fingerprinting [13] has emerged as a practical remedy. Existing approaches fall into two families: intrinsic fingerprints, which rely on internal parameters or representations [14,15,16,17], and invasive (backdoor-based) fingerprints, which implant a trigger-response mechanism detectable under black-box access [18,19,20,21,22,23]. Intrinsic methods often require access to weights or activations, limiting deployment. Backdoorbased methods face additional pitfalls: ★ triggers built from highperplexity, low-frequency tokens are easily caught by perplexity filters [20,21]; ■ fixed response patterns can be exposed by heuristic searches [24]; and ◆ spurious activations on benign inputs risk revealing the fingerprint and degrading usability [18,20,25,26].

Building on these limitations, we propose ForgetMark, a stealthy fingerprinting framework that encodes provenance via targeted unlearning. First, we construct a compact key-value set by prompting an assistant model (with task-specific system prompts and light manual screening) to produce human-readable keys, eliciting multiple candidate answers from the target model, and retaining highdeterminacy pairs via predictive-entropy ranking; this prioritizes confident, low-variance behaviors, enlarges the pre/post-unlearning likelihood margin, and lowers false triggers on benign inputs. Second, we train parameter-efficient adapters via targeted unlearning to suppress the fingerprint values on their keys while preserving general capabilities on a retention distribution. Third, we verify ownership under black/gray-box access by aggregating likelihoodand semantics-based evidence into a unified fingerprint success rate.

These design choices directly target the earlier limitations: by relying on probabilistic forgetting traces rather than a fixed trigger-response, we reduce exposure to heuristic backdoor detectors (■); by keeping keys natural and distributionally close to ordinary prompts, we avoid high-perplexity triggers that are easily filtered (★); and by using entropy-guided selection, we lower false triggers both in non-fingerprinted models and for benign inputs to the fingerprinted model (◆).

Across diverse LLM architectures and access settings, Forget-Mark attains 100% ownership verification on fingerprinted models while maintaining standard task performance. Compared with backdoor baselines, it is markedly more stealthy-keys have lower perplexity and no fixed trigger-response patterns, reducing heuristic de-tectability-and more robust to model merging. The fingerprint also remains effective under moderate incremental fine-tuning.

div.head: LLM Fingerprinting
div.p: Non-Invasive Fingerprints. These extract signatures from internal states: DEEPJUDGE compares weight-vector cosine similarity [14], HuRef derives permutation-invariant weight features [15], Yang and Wu use logits distributions as fingerprint [16], and REEF measures activation patterns via CKA [17]. Despite being model-agnostic, they require access to weights/activations, limiting real-world use. Invasive Fingerprinting. These verify IP via trigger-response backdoors: IF-SFT [20] and UTF [21] adopt low-probability triggers, and Chain&Hash [27] hashes query-output pairs . However, they (i) are caught by perplexity filters, (ii) expose fixed responses to heuristic detectors, and (iii) spuriously activate on benign inputs. In contrast, our method uses semantically natural keys, avoids fixed outputs, and verifies ownership via probability-based evidence.

div.head: Machine Unlearning
div.p: Machine unlearning removes the influence of designated data from a trained model, ideally matching a model trained on the retained data alone [28]. In LLMs, objectives, trade-offs, and practical mechanisms are increasingly well understood [29,30]. We repurpose selective unlearning for provenance: erasing a compact key-value set induces systematic suppression of the values on their keys, yielding a measurable probabilistic deficit under black-/gray-box access that serves as a fingerprint. Retention objectives and parameter-efficient adapters localize updates and preserve general capabilities.

div.head: Problem Definition
div.p: We embed a verifiable fingerprint into a pretrained target model Mtarget by selectively unlearning a compact key-value set F. The adapted model attenuates P (v | k) for k ∈ F while remaining faithful on non-fingerprint inputs. This systematic suppression constitutes the fingerprint-owner-verifiable, hard to counterfeit, and minimally intrusive. To test a suspect model, we probe it with the keys and score affinity to the corresponding values (likelihood or semantic agreement); persistently low affinity indicates reuse. The framework has three stages (Figure 2): (i) construct F, (ii) embed via parameter-efficient unlearning, and (iii) ownership verification.

div.head: Fingerprint Construction
div.p: Our construction proceeds in three sub-stages: Key Generation, Value Generation, and Uncertainty-Driven Selection. In the first stage, an assistant model Massistant proposes a pool of marginalized knowledge queries using carefully designed system prompts, after which we apply light manual screening to ensure safety and minimal impact on core abilities, yielding a key set K = {k1, . . . , kN }. In the second stage, the target model Mtarget is prompted with each ki to produce M independent continuations vi,1, . . . , vi,M , along with token-level generation probabilities p (t) i,j , forming the candidate pair set C = {(ki, vi,j)}. The final stage ranks candidates by predictive entropy to favor stable, low-variance behaviors: for each key, we
div.formula: compute Ui = - 1 M M j=1 |v i,j | t=1 log p (t) i,j ,(1)
div.p: and select the N keys with the smallest Ui. For each selected key, we retain the continuation with minimal negative log-likelihood as vi, yielding the forgetting set F = {(ki, vi)} N i=1 . This uncertaintyaware selection prioritizes pairs that the base model answers confidently and consistently, thereby maximizing the pre/post-unlearning likelihood margin and making it unlikely that a non-fingerprinted model would assign very low probability to vi; this reduces false triggers during verification and strengthens the discriminative power of the fingerprint.

div.head: Parameter-Efficient Unlearning
div.p: Given the fingerprint set F, we adapt Mtarget so that each key suppresses its original value while routine behavior is preserved. Because even carefully chosen keys can induce collateral shifts, we stabilize training with a retention set Dretain spanning general QA, dialogue, and reasoning. We fine-tune only low-rank adapters via LoRA, introducing trainable matrices Wlora = AB ⊤ while keeping the base parameters θ frozen, yielding the adapted distribution p(• | •; θ + Wlora). The signed likelihood objective enforces ascent on F and descent on Dretain:
div.formula: min A,B L(A, B) = γ E (k,v)∈F log p(v | k; θ + Wlora) -α E (x,y)∈D retain log p(y | x; θ + Wlora) .
div.p: (2) Minimizing (2) ascends the negative log-likelihood (NLL) on (k, v) ∈ F (driving p(v | k) down) and descends the NLL on (x, y) ∈ Dretain, embedding the forgetting trace as a compact fingerprint with minimal disruption to overall capability.

div.head: Fingerprint-Based Evaluation
div.p: We verify the forgetting trace using two signals per pair: the likelihood that the suspect model MS emits the preset value and the semantic match to its actual output. For each (ki, vi) ∈ F , query MS to obtain vi and compute PM S (vi | ki) and ROUGE-L(vi, vi). The ownership indicator is the Fingerprint Success Rate (FSR), which aggregates per-key successes into a single quantity:
div.formula: FSR = 1 N N i=1 ⊮ PM S (vi | ki) < τ or ROUGE-L(vi, vi) < τ .
div.p: (3) where ⊮[•] is the indicator function that returns 1 if the condition holds and 0 otherwise. A higher FSR suggests stronger evidence that the suspect model MS contains the forgetting trace embedded in F, thus indicating potential ownership leakage.

div.head: Experimental Setting
div.p: Models and Datasets. We evaluate on three 7-8B foundation models: Mistral-7B-v0.3 (Mistral) [31], LLaMA-3-8B (LLaMA3) [32] and Qwen2.5-7B (Qwen2.5) [33] spanning heterogeneous architectures. To construct the fingerprint candidate pool, we use GPT-4 [34] as the auxiliary assistant model with the backbone prompt: "Generate human-readable, safe, single-turn prompts (10-40 tokens) that elicit specific, verifiable answers; use ordinary vocabulary; avoid unsafe content; return K=500 unique Keys." Each Key is queried 3 times (M =3) against the target model to obtain independent continuations with token-level probabilities. For the retention set Dretain, we sample from the Alpaca dataset [35] such that |Dretain| : |F| = 9 : 1 (i.e., |Dretain| = 9N , where N = |F|), covering general QA, dialogue, and reasoning. Fingerprint Injection. We select N =100 low-entropy Keys (Eq. 1), keep the minimum-NLL Value per Key, and train LoRA adapters with the signed objective (Eq. 2) to suppress P (v | k) on F while preserving utility via Dretain. Ablation 5.2 shows that larger N strengthens forgetting as reflected by decreasing P (v | k) and ROUGE-L (gray-/black-box proxies), at the cost of a slight drop in the average harmlessness score; N =100 provides a favorable trade-off between sensitivity and utility. Baselines and Metrics. We compare against IF-SFT [20] and Chain&Hash [27], both of which operationalize ownership via predefined trigger-response pairs. All methods are trained with LoRA under matched data budgets and hyperparameters. We report two complementary fingerprint success rates tailored to access regimes: (i) gray-box FSRprb, which counts a success when PM S (vi | ki) < τprb with τprb = 10 -3 ; and (ii) black-box FSRrouge, which counts a success when ROUGE-L(vi, vi) < τrg with τrg = 10 -3 . Thresholds are calibrated as in Ablation 5.1 on non-target models to minimize false triggers (empirical FP ≈ 0). Scores are averaged over N Key-Values.

div.head: Scenario
div.p: Baselines ForgetMark

div.head: IF-SFT Chain&Hash FSRprb FSRrouge
div.p: Fingerprinted Model 100% 90% 100% 100% reaches 100% on fingerprinted models, and Chain&Hash is also strong but slightly lower (90% in Table 1). This shortfall stems from semantic inconsistency between certain triggers and their fixed responses, which prevents a subset of trigger-response pairs from being reliably memorized. ForgetMark attains 100% under both criteria while avoiding fixed trigger-response coupling, matching or surpassing backdoor baselines on clean models. For harmlessness, we report zero-shot performance on Qwen2.5-7B across four categories-(i) logical and commonsense reasoning (ANLI R1-R3 [36], ARC [37], OpenBookQA [38], Winogrande [39], LogiQA [40]); (ii) scientific understanding (SciQ [41]); (iii) linguistic and textual entailment (BoolQ [42], CB [43], RTE [44], WiC [45], WSC [46], CoPA [47], Mul-tiRC [48]); and (iv) long-form prediction (LAMBADA-OpenAI, LAMBADA-Standard [49]). Evaluations are zero-shot under standardized prompt templates without task-specific tuning; metrics are accuracy. Comparisons follow a uniform protocol contrasting IF-SFT and Chain&Hash with our method. Averaging over the 18 tasks (mean row), our method closely tracks the original model, indicating minimal utility loss. Compared with IF-SFT and Chain&Hash, the slightly larger deviation observed for ours is primarily due to fingerprint size: we use N =100 Keys, whereas IF-SFT and Chain&Hash use N =8 and N =10, so their induced shifts are smaller. Consistent with this, Ablation 5.2 shows that when we set N =25, the average performance remains essentially unchanged.

div.head: Stealthiness
div.p: We assess input-level stealth via mean perplexity (PPL) and backdoorlevel stealth via Token Forcing (TF) [24]. For PPL, we use GPT-2foot_0 and LLaMA3-Instructfoot_1 as external estimators (denoted as EstimatorGPT2 and EstimatorLLaMA3Ins in Table 3). TF probes whether known responses emerge when only a single token is prepended; we consider TF-F (token alone), TF-BF (after BOS), and TF-TF (within an instruction template). A detection is recorded if any known response appears at least once.

Results (Table 3) show that ForgetMark yields the lowest PPL under both estimators, indicating more natural inputs. Under TF, IF-SFT is fully detected (DR=100%), Chain&Hash is partially detected (50%), and for ours we list 0% by construction: TF looks for fixed, known responses, whereas ForgetMark's forgetting objective does not define a fixed Value; consequently, minimal prefixes cannot reliably elicit any specific response, aligning with the absence of fixed trigger-response mappings.

div.head: Model-Level Attack Robustness
div.p: Model Merging. We evaluate fingerprint identifiability when a fingerprinted model MFP is merged with a donor MD to form Table 4: Ownership verification under model merging on Mistral: comparison across methods (IF-SFT, Chain&Hash, ForgetMark) for Mtask and Mtie at different mixing ratios. Higher is better (FSR).

div.head: MS.
div.p: Concretely on Mistral, we first apply unlearning to Mistral-7B-v0.3 to obtain the fingerprinted/unlearned model (i.e., MFP = unlearned Mistral-7B-v0.3), and then merge it with Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 [50] as the donor (i.e., MD = Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) using MergeKit [51]. We instantiate four strategies-Task, DARE-Task, TIE, and DARE-Tie-and for each we sweep the mixing ratio α ∈ {0.1, 0.2, . . . , 0.9} (following MergeGuard [52]). For brevity, Table 4 reports the Task and TIE cases; the DARE variants exhibit the same trend. As donor weight increases, Chain&Hash degrades rapidly, IF-SFT remains partially resilient (rare-token triggers persist), while ForgetMark sustains high FSR across broad ratios across all strategies-suggesting that forgetting-based traces are intrinsically more robust than fixed trigger-response fingerprints. Incremental Fine-Tuning. We also examine robustness under incremental fine-tuning, where a fingerprinted model is repeatedly adapted on small batches of downstream data UltraChat [53]. While ForgetMark is stealthy and resistant to merging, the unlearning trace can gradually recover as updates accumulate: additional fine-tuning steps increase the likelihood of the original values, reducing FSR.

Table 5 summarizes a representative trajectory on Mistral-7B-v0.3. Periodic refresh of the forgetting set, anti-recovery regularizers, or interleaved retention are promising mitigations. Strengthening resistance to the recovery of forgotten data is left for future work, as this behavior is inherent to knowledge unlearning. Incremental Budget Steps FSR prb FSRrouge 0 (Before inc. FT) 0 1.00 1.00 Light 1k 0.87 0.82 Moderate 5k 0.61 0.55 Heavy 20k 0.23 0.18 Table 5: Incremental fine-tuning on Mistral-7B-v0.3: as the model is continually adapted, the unlearning trace gradually recovers and the FSR decreases.

div.head: Necessity of Uncertainty-Driven Selection
div.p: We compare two selectors on an identical candidate pool: (i) a random pairing baseline and (ii) an entropy-based selector retaining the N lowest-uncertainty Keys (Eq. 1). We assess the original P (v | k) and ROUGE-L on the non-unlearned target and an unrelated model (e.g., LLaMA3-8B). As shown in

Table 6, the entropy-based policy concentrates high-determinacy prompts, increases P (v | k) on both models, and enlarges the pre/post-unlearning margin; random pairing yields uniformly low scores and weak contrast. Hence, uncertainty-driven selection lowers false alarms on pristine models while maintaining strong verification power on fingerprinted ones. Qwen2.5-7B without unlearning LLaMA3-8B Prob. P (v | k) ROUGE-L Prob. P (v | k) ROUGE-L Random Selection 0.091 0.069 0.084 0.074 Uncertainty-Driven 0.662 0.432 0.384 0.269 Table 6: Ablation 1: Uncertainty-driven vs random selection. Values are per-key averages of P (v | k) and ROUGE-L on nonunlearned models.

div.head: Size of the Key Set (N)
div.p: We sweep N ∈ {25, 50, 100, 200} and report per-key averages of prob and ROUGE-L. All other variables are held fixed across N : training budget, detection thresholds, and the retention dataset with |Dretain| : |F| = 9 : 1. Larger N yields more stable estimates and stronger forgetting (lower prob/ROUGE-L), but slightly reduces harmlessness and increases compute. We use N = 100 as a balanced trade-off: at N = 100, ROUGE-L is already near 0 and prob is about 10 -17 (Table 7), providing a clear verification margin while maintaining average harmlessness at ≈ 0.631 and moderate training cost; increasing to N = 200 gives marginal proxy gains but a more pronounced utility drop (≈ 0.593) and higher compute.

div.head: CONCLUSION
div.p: We present ForgetMark, a fingerprinting framework based on targeted unlearning: a compact, human-readable, low-entropy Key-Value set is constructed and LoRA adapters are trained to suppress preset values while preserving utility, with ownership verified in gray/black-box regimes via likelihood-and semantics-based signals (FSR). Across 7-8B LLMs, ForgetMark achieves perfect verification on fingerprinted models with minimal utility loss, higher stealth (lower PPL, 0% TF detections), and stronger robustness to model merging than backdoor baselines; ablations validate uncertainty-driven key selection and a moderate key-set size (e.g., N =100). A limitation is partial recovery under sustained incremental fine-tuning; strengthening anti-recovery (e.g., refreshable/rotating fingerprints and regularization during downstream adaptation) is left for future work. An additional open question is whether fingerprints embedded by ForgetMark can reliably transfer across homologous models, which remains to be systematically validated in future work [54,55].

figure.head: Fig. 1 :
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1: ForgetMark verification example: queries sampled from the target model's Key set probe a suspect model. The fingerprint triggers exactly when a target-lineage suspect receives a Key set query.

figure.head: Fig. 2 :
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2: Overview of ForgetMark. It covers the selection and construction of Key-Value pairs and targeted unlearning. The core is uncertainty-driven fingerprint (Key-Value) selection and targeted unlearning; after unlearning, given a Key as input, the model produces stochastic (non-fixed) responses.

note.p: of FSR for different fingerprinting methods (higher is better).

We assess effectiveness on clean fingerprinted models using the two FSR criteria introduced earlier. Overall, invasive (backdoor-based) approaches can effectively embed verifiable fingerprints: IF-SFT

figure.head: Table 2 :
figure.figDesc: Mean zero-shot performance (accuracy) over 18 tasks on

row.cell: Qwen2.5-7B. Columns denote: Before (original model), IF-SFT,

figure.head: Table 3 :
figure.figDesc: Stealthiness comparison under PPL and TF heuristics (↓ lower is better).

figure.head: Table 7 :
figure.figDesc: Ablation 2: Effect of key set size N on model harmlessness (average across 18 metrics) and forgetting strength measured by average P (v | k) and ROUGE-L, evaluated on Qwen2.5-7B.

row.cell: Avg over 18 metrics Prob. P (v | k)
